64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67

73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84

89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...

95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```

105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.

107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149

151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)

212

223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43

46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).

48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so

51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).

54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE (iter-007): blocked on absent tensor/monoidal structure for SheafOfModules.
160	  % The degree-m component is Gamma(X_s, L_s^{otimes m}); Mathlib at the pinned commit has no
161	  % MonoidalCategoryStruct on X.Modules (synthesis fails) and no SheafOfModules/PresheafOfModules
162	  % tensor lemmas, so L^{otimes m} cannot even be named. This is a deep infrastructure prerequisite
163	  % (tensor product of sheaves of modules + lax-monoidal global sections), to be blueprinted as a
164	  % separate multi-lemma sub-build. See task_results .../QuotScheme.md.
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
166	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
167	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
168	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
169	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
170	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
171	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
172	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
173	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
174	  %  the function
175	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
176	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
177	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
178	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
179	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
180	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
181	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
182	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
183	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
184	  \[
185	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
186	  \]
187	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
188	  the tensor product of sections
189	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
190	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
191	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
192	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
193	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
194	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
195	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
196	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
197	  polynomial ring).
198	\end{definition}

200	\begin{definition}
201	[Section graded module]
202	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
204	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
205	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
206	  % Polynomials" (read from
207	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
210	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
211	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
212	  %  the function
213	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
214	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
215	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
216	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
217	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
218	  I.7.}
219	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
220	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
221	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
222	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
223	  \[
224	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
225	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
226	  \]
227	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
228	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
229	  by the section tensor product
230	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
231	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
232	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
233	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
234	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
235	\end{definition}
236	
237	\begin{lemma}
238	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
239	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
241	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
242	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
243	  % Polynomials" (read from
244	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
245	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
246	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
247	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
248	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
249	  %  the function
250	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
251	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
252	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
253	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
254	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
255	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
256	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
257	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
258	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
259	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
260	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
261	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
262	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
263	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
264	\end{lemma}
265	
266	\begin{proof}
267	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
268	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
269	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
270	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
271	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
272	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
273	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
274	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
275	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
276	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
277	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
278	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
279	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
280	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
281	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
282	\end{proof}
283	
284	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
285	  graded series]
286	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
287	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
288	  \mathlibok
289	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
290	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
291	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
292	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
293	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
294	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
295	  equals \(h(n)\):
296	  \[
297	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
298	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
299	  \]
300	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
301	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
302	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
303	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
304	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
305	\end{lemma}
306	
307	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
308	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
310	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule, lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
311	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
312	  % Polynomials" (read from
313	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L459-L464).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
315	  % "$${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
316	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
317	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
318	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that $\chi(F(m))$
319	  %  is indeed a polynomial in $m$ under the above assumption is a special
320	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof)."
321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Hilbert--Serre,
322	  [Hartshorne], I.7.}
323	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
324	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
325	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
326	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
327	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
328	  such that for all \(n > N\)
329	  \[
330	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
331	  \]
332	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
333	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
334	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\). Applied to \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and
335	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (finitely generated by
336	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), this exhibits the graded Hilbert function
337	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
338	  as the eventual coefficient function of such a rational series.
339	
340	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
341	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
342	  module). It is a project-side obligation: Mathlib's
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} supplies only the converse
344	  polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not the production of the
345	  rational series from the module.
346	\end{lemma}
347	
348	\begin{proof}
349	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
350	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the ring \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) is Noetherian
351	  and finitely generated over \(\kappa = \kappa(s)\), say by homogeneous
352	  generators \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) of positive degrees, and
353	  \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
354	  with finite-dimensional components. Passing to a Veronese subalgebra and
355	  re-grading reduces to the case where \(R\) is generated in degree \(1\), so
356	  \(R\) is a graded quotient of a polynomial ring
357	  \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\). One proves rationality of the Hilbert series by
358	  induction on \(r\): multiplication by \(x_r\) gives a short exact sequence of
359	  graded modules
360	  \(0 \to K \to M(-1) \xrightarrow{x_r} M \to C \to 0\)
361	  with \(K, C\) finitely generated graded modules annihilated by \(x_r\), hence
362	  modules over a polynomial ring in \(r - 1\) variables; additivity of
363	  \(\dim_\kappa\) on the graded components turns the inductive hypothesis for
364	  \(K, C\) into the statement that
365	  \((1 - X) \cdot \sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n\) is, up to a polynomial, of the
366	  form \(q \cdot (1 - X)^{-(d-1)}\), whence
367	  \(\sum_n (\dim_\kappa M_n) X^n = p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) up to a polynomial. The
368	  base case \(r = 0\) has \(M\) finite-dimensional, so \(\dim_\kappa M_n = 0\) for
369	  \(n \gg 0\) and the series is a polynomial. Choosing \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\)
370	  (the integer coefficients viewed in \(\mathbb{Q}\)) gives the claim.
371	\end{proof}

373	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
374	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
376	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
377	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
378	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
379	  % Polynomials" (read from
380	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
381	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
382	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
383	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
384	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
385	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
386	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
387	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
388	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
389	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
390	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
391	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
392	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
393	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
394	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
395	  \[
396	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
397	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
398	  \]
399	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
400	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
401	\end{theorem}
402	
403	\begin{proof}
404	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
405	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
406	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
407	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
408	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components. By
409	  \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
410	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
411	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
412	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
413	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
414	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
415	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
416	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
417	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
418	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
419	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
420	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
421	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
422	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
423	\end{proof}

430	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support

499	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
500	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}

502	  \mathlibok
503	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
504	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
505	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
506	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
507	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
508	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
509	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
510	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.

751	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
752	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
753	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
754	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
755	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
756	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
757	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
758	  %  [...]
759	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
760	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
761	  %  [...]
762	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
763	  %  the determinant line bundle
764	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
765	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
766	  %  [...]
767	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
768	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
769	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
770	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
771	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
772	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
773	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
774	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
775	  Explained Ch.~5).}
776	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
777	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
778	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
779	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
780	  equipped with a tautological quotient
781	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
782	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
783	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
784	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
785	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
786	
787	\end{theorem}
788	
789	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
790	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
791	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
792	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
793	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
794	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
795	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
796	\begin{proof}
797	  
798	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
799	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
800	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
801	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
802	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
803	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
804	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
805	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
806	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
807	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
808	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
809	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
810	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
811	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
812	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
813	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
814	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
815	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
816	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
817	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
818	
819	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
820	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
821	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
822	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
823	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
824	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
825	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
826	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
827	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
828	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
829	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
830	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
831	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
832	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
833	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
834	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
835	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
836	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
837	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
838	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
839	\end{proof}
840	
841	\section{Out of scope}
842	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
843	
844	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
845	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
846	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
847	\begin{itemize}
848	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
849	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
850	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
851	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
852	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
853	    project-side substrate -- not constructed in this chapter.
854	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
855	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
856	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
857	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
858	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
859	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
860	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
861	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
862	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
863	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
864	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
865	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
866	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
867	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
868	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
869	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
870	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
871	    subproject.
872	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
873	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
874	    constructed in this chapter.
875	\end{itemize}

593	\noindent
594	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. We will define
595	a presheaf $\widetilde M$ on the basis of standard opens.
596	Suppose that $U \subset \Spec(R)$ is a standard open.
597	If $f, g \in R$ are such that $D(f) = D(g)$, then
598	by Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above there are canonical
599	maps $M_f \to M_g$ and $M_g \to M_f$ which are mutually inverse.
600	Hence we may choose any $f$ such that $U = D(f)$
601	and define
602	$$
603	\widetilde M(U) = M_f.
604	$$
605	Note that if $D(g) \subset D(f)$, then by
606	Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above we have
607	a canonical map
608	$$
609	\widetilde M(D(f)) = M_f \longrightarrow M_g = \widetilde M(D(g)).
610	$$
611	Clearly, this defines a presheaf of abelian groups on the basis
612	of standard opens. If $M = R$, then $\widetilde R$ is a presheaf
613	of rings on the basis of standard opens.
614	
615	\medskip\noindent
616	Let us compute the stalk of $\widetilde M$ at a point $x \in \Spec(R)$.
617	Suppose that $x$ corresponds to the prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$.
618	By definition of the stalk we see that
619	$$
620	\widetilde M_x = \colim_{f\in R, f\not\in \mathfrak p} M_f
621	$$
622	Here the set $\{f \in R, f \not \in \mathfrak p\}$ is preordered by
623	the rule $f \geq f' \Leftrightarrow D(f) \subset D(f')$.
624	If $f_1, f_2 \in R \setminus \mathfrak p$, then we have
625	$f_1f_2 \geq f_1$ in this ordering. Hence by
626	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-localization-colimit}
627	we conclude that
628	$$
629	\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}.
630	$$
631	
632	\medskip\noindent
633	Next, we check the sheaf condition for the standard open coverings.
634	If $D(f) = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(g_i)$, then the sheaf condition
635	for this covering is equivalent with the exactness of the
636	sequence
637	$$
638	0 \to M_f \to \bigoplus M_{g_i} \to \bigoplus M_{g_ig_j}.
639	$$
640	Note that $D(g_i) = D(fg_i)$, and hence we can rewrite this
641	sequence as the sequence
642	$$
643	0 \to M_f \to \bigoplus M_{fg_i} \to \bigoplus M_{fg_ig_j}.
644	$$
645	In addition, by Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} above
646	we see that $g_1, \ldots, g_n$ generate the unit ideal
647	in $R_f$. Thus we may apply
648	Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}
649	to the module $M_f$ over $R_f$ and the elements $g_1, \ldots, g_n$.
650	We conclude that the sequence is exact. By the remarks
651	made above, we see that $\widetilde M$ is a sheaf
652	on the basis of standard opens.
653	
654	\medskip\noindent
655	Thus we conclude from the material in
656	Sheaves, Section \ref{sheaves-section-bases}
657	that there exists a
658	unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
659	which agrees with $\widetilde R$ on the standard opens.
660	Note that by our computation of stalks above, the
661	stalks of this sheaf of rings are all local rings.
662	
663	\medskip\noindent
664	Similarly, for any $R$-module $M$ there exists
665	a unique sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
666	$\mathcal{F}$ which agrees with $\widetilde M$ on the
667	standard opens, see
668	Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-extend-off-basis-module}.
669	
670	\begin{definition}
671	\label{definition-structure-sheaf}
672	Let $R$ be a ring.
673	\begin{enumerate}
674	\item The {\it structure sheaf $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ of the
675	spectrum of $R$} is the unique sheaf of rings $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$
676	which agrees with $\widetilde R$ on the basis of standard opens.
677	\item The locally ringed space
678	$(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)})$ is called
679	the {\it spectrum} of $R$ and denoted $\Spec(R)$.
680	\item The sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
681	extending $\widetilde M$ to all opens of $\Spec(R)$
682	is called the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
683	associated to $M$. This sheaf is denoted $\widetilde M$ as
684	well.
685	\end{enumerate}
686	\end{definition}
687	
688	\noindent
689	We summarize the results obtained so far.
690	
691	\begin{lemma}
692	\label{lemma-spec-sheaves}
693	Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
694	be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
695	associated to $M$.
696	\begin{enumerate}
697	\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R$.
698	\item We have $\Gamma(\Spec(R), \widetilde M) = M$ as an $R$-module.
699	\item For every $f \in R$ we have
700	$\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}) = R_f$.
701	\item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
702	as an $R_f$-module.
703	\item Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
704	on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
705	are the maps
706	$R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
707	\ref{lemma-standard-open}.
708	\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
709	be the corresponding point. We have
710	$\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R), x} = R_{\mathfrak p}$.
711	\item Let $\mathfrak p$ be a prime of $R$, and let $x \in \Spec(R)$
712	be the corresponding point. We have $\widetilde M_x = M_{\mathfrak p}$
713	as an $R_{\mathfrak p}$-module.
714	\end{enumerate}
715	Moreover, all these identifications are functorial in the $R$
716	module $M$. In particular, the functor $M \mapsto \widetilde M$
717	is an exact functor from the category of $R$-modules
718	to the category of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules.
719	\end{lemma}
720	
721	\begin{proof}
722	Assertions (1) - (7) are clear from the discussion above.

input.new_string:
\textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the construction is a
  project-built primitive.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
  \(U \subseteq X\),
  \[
    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
  \]
  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).

  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
  hypotheses enter. The bridge
  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.

  \emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
\end{definition}

input.new_string:
\emph{Note.} This is a project-built primitive; Mathlib does not carry an
  annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
  localization]
  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
  \[
    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
  \]
  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).

  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).

  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
  %  associated to $M$.
  %  [...]
  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
  %  as an $R_f$-module."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on \(X\)
  that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type} (over a locally noetherian
  base, matching the coherence encoding under which \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) is a
  finite \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module on each affine open). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
    \item the section-restriction map
      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
  \end{enumerate}
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).

  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf of finite type is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
  finite \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
  \(N\). The Spec-local computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
  this identification across the affine isomorphism
  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
\end{proof}
